attributes.stem: A 78-year-old man is admitted under General Medicine for management of an exacerbation of his chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. His other health issues include hypertension and benign prostate hyperplasia. Five days after admission, at 2am, he complains of worsening suprapubic pain. Up to that time, he had been making slow progress and the nursing charts show that he had not used his bowels for four days. On review of his charts, he has not been taking his regular medications. On examination, he is haemodynamically stable, but is confused and not oriented to place and person. There is suprapubic tenderness with dullness to percussion. A bladder scan shows a residual urine volume of 1200ml. There is no Advance Care Directive. Attempts have been made to contact the family via phone which have been unsuccessful.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Continue to contact next of kin and obtain consent prior to urethral catheterisation
Administer analgesia and obtain consent for urethral catheterisation when pain has settled
Urine dipstick and commence prophylactic antibiotics
attributes.explanation:
Acute urinary retention is a medical emergency with the most common cause being benign prostate hyperplasia in men. Urgent urethral catheterisation is warranted to resolve the retention, alleviate the patient's symptoms and likely improve his delirium. Though consent is not able to be acquired due to the patient's impaired decision-making, when urgent treatment is warranted and informed consent is not possible, consent is then acquired through next of kin or the input of a second doctor. In the situation where failed attempts have been made to contact the family, given the fact that this is an emergency and further delay could cause harm to the patient (such as acute kidney injury and worsening delirium), minor procedures such as urethral catheterisation can be provided by 2 doctor consent if it meets the following criteria (taken from consent to medical treatment and palliative care act 1995) of:

  • The medical practitioner who is to administer the treatment believes it is necessary to meet an immediate risk to the patient's life or health. A second medical practitioner who has personally examined the patient gives a written opinion agreeing with the first medical practitioner unless it is not reasonably practicable to do so.

  • The patient (if of or over 16 years of age) has not, to the best of the medical practitioner's knowledge, refused to consent to the treatment.

  • The medical practitioner who is to administer the treatment has made reasonable inquiries to ascertain whether the patient (if of or over 18 years of age) has given an advance care directive, unless it is not reasonable practicable to do so.

In the rare circumstance of family refusal for inappropriate reasons, it would require escalation to an urgent guardianship hearing.

Whilst giving this patient analgesics for his pain might be helpful, the most effective pain relief will obtained once his bladder has been decompressed. Treatment should be directed towards the causes such as possible missed medication for BPH and constipation. For example, aperients may be considered if constipation is suspected as a contributing cause of retention. However, in this case, the patient is in considerable discomfort and needs prompt relief of his symptoms - and that is best achieved by bladder catheterisation. Urine dipstick should also be performed as a urinary tract infection should be excluded. However, these can be performed at a later time.
learningPoints: Consent is generally not required where the patient lacks capacity and immediate treatment is necessary to save a person’s life or prevent serious injury to their health.
In cases of acute urinary retention, prompt urethral catheterisation is necessary to relieve symptoms and prevent complications.
Delirium can be exacerbated by untreated acute urinary retention, and addressing the underlying cause can improve mental status.
references: Legal Services Commission South Australia. Jul 28th 2014. [Internet]. Emergency medical or dental treatment. Accessed on 8 Jan 2025. Available from: https://lawhandbook.sa.gov.au/ch29s02s02s01.php.

Dougherty JM, Aeddula NR. Male Urinary Retention. [Updated 2022 Aug 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538499/

Fitzpatrick JM, Desgrandchamps F, Adjali K, Gomez Guerra L, Hong SJ, El Khalid S, Ratana-Olarn K; Reten-World Study Group. Management of acute urinary retention: a worldwide survey of 6074 men with benign prostatic hyperplasia. BJU Int. 2012 Jan;109(1):88-95. doi: 10.1111/j.1464-410X.2011.10430.x. Epub 2011 Nov 25. PMID: 22117624; PMCID: PMC3272343. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22117624/
